data: Iscrizioni latine lapidarie del Museo di Palermo
Livia Bivona, Iscrizioni latine lapidarie del Museo di Palermo, Sikelia 5 (Palermo: Flaccovio, 1970)

Large marble plaque, broken in fragments, which have been recomposed in a plaster seting. The lower right corner is lost, and several letters in the first two lines have been large restored, but the text is complete.
Found in Tyndaris, in close proximity to a statue of Trajan.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 2022-05-23
Partially reopened following extensive restoration work. Comprehensive collection of archaeological material, predominantly from the West of the island, with an important Etruscan collection.
Centre of Palermo, Piazza Olivella 24, 90100 - Palermo

Facsimile Images

Apparatus criticus

  • Text of IMusPalermo

English translation

Alfredo Tosques

Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the seventh time, acclaimed imperator for the fourth time, consul for the fifth time, father of the fatherland.

Italian translation

Alfredo Tosques

L’imperatore Cesare, figlio del divo Nerva, Nerva Traiano Augusto Germanico Dacico, pontefice massimo, investito della potestà tribunizia per la settima volta, imperatore per la quarta volta, console per la quinta volta, padre della patria.

commentary

Roman imperial dedications normally follow a very rigid structure, with the emperor and his full titulature in the dative case at the beginning, followed by the dedicants in the nominative at the end of the text. In this inscription, however, the Emperor appears unusually in the nominative, with no dedicants mentioned. This may indicate that the inscription commemorated an act of imperial euergetism (public benefaction) carried out by the Emperor himself. If this were the case, as the agent rather than the dedicatee, the use of the nominative would be explained.

Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII imperator IIII consul V pater patriae
Text of IMusPalermo

Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the seventh time, acclaimed imperator for the fourth time, consul for the fifth time, father of the fatherland.

L’imperatore Cesare, figlio del divo Nerva, Nerva Traiano Augusto Germanico Dacico, pontefice massimo, investito della potestà tribunizia per la settima volta, imperatore per la quarta volta, console per la quinta volta, padre della patria.

Roman imperial dedications normally follow a very rigid structure, with the emperor and his full titulature in the dative case at the beginning, followed by the dedicants in the nominative at the end of the text. In this inscription, however, the Emperor appears unusually in the nominative, with no dedicants mentioned. This may indicate that the inscription commemorated an act of imperial euergetism (public benefaction) carried out by the Emperor himself. If this were the case, as the agent rather than the dedicatee, the use of the nominative would be explained.
